configure-and-initialize-a-repository.label: Initialize Your First Repository
configure-and-initialize-a-repository.summary: Create a repository in the correct folder, confirm its initial state, and keep the author identity local to that project.

sketch0.title: Create the Repository in the Right Place

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Initialize an Ordinary Folder
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

The folder already contains a README but has no history. Initialize Git with `main` as the first branch. This exercise asks for only the initialization step.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Choose the initial branch while Git creates the repository metadata.

help.concept: `git init -b main` creates repository metadata in the current folder and names its first branch in one step.
help.hint_1: The terminal is already inside the project folder, so do not change directories.
help.hint_2: The task asks for one action: initialize this folder with the requested first branch.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

The folder already contains a README but has no history. Initialize Git with `main` as the first branch. This exercise asks for only the initialization step.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you initialize this project folder with `main` as its first branch?

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Confirm the New Repository
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

Git has already initialized this folder on `main`. Use the full repository report to confirm three facts: the current branch is `main`, no commits exist yet, and `README.md` is still untracked.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Ask Git for the detailed repository report; this step should only inspect the project.

help.concept: The full status report confirms the branch, whether history exists, and which files are still untracked without changing repository state.
help.hint_1: Look for the branch line and the message explaining that no commits exist yet.
help.hint_2: The same report should list `README.md` under untracked files.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

Git has already initialized this folder on `main`. Use the full repository report to confirm three facts: the current branch is `main`, no commits exist yet, and `README.md` is still untracked.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C trail-journal init -q -b main

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C trail-journal init -q -b main

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you ask Git for the full repository report and confirm the branch, empty history, and untracked README?

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Set a Repository-Only Identity
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

Set the author name to `ZoeSkoul Learner` and the email to `learner@zoeskoul.local` inside `trail-journal/` only. Print both local values afterward.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Keep the author identity inside this repository rather than changing machine-wide settings.

help.concept: Repository-local identity applies only to this project and does not alter the machine-wide Git configuration.
help.hint_1: Both the name and email need repository-local values.
help.hint_2: Read the stored values back to catch spelling or scope mistakes.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.solutionCode:
git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
git config --local --get user.name
git config --local --get user.email
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

Set the author name to `ZoeSkoul Learner` and the email to `learner@zoeskoul.local` inside `trail-journal/` only. Print both local values afterward.

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of Version Control
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of version control systems like Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on `main`, inspect the metadata Git created, and keep the author identity local to that repository.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To track changes and collaborate on projects

sketch0.title: Create the Repository in the Right Place
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
A Git repository belongs to one project folder. Initialize too high and unrelated files become part of the same project; initialize too low and part of the project is left outside.

Once the terminal is inside the intended folder, create the repository and name its first branch in one step:

```bash
git init -b main
```

Git adds private metadata without changing the visible project files or creating a commit. This command is taught here once; later projects will reuse it without another introduction.

sketch1.title: Confirm the Initial State
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Initialization creates a repository, but not history. The safest confirmation is Git's full report:

```bash
git status
```

Immediately after initialization, the report identifies `main`, explains that no commits exist yet, and lists project files that are still untracked. It only reads the repository; it does not stage or record anything.

This is the one full introduction to `git status`. Later lessons will use it as evidence rather than explain it again.

sketch2.title: Set the Repository Identity
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
Every commit records an author name and email. In a learning workspace, keep those values inside the current repository:

```bash
git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
```

Read the values back with `git config --local --get ...` before the first commit. The `--local` scope protects machine-wide settings and makes the project self-contained.
